However, paragraphing is faulty: paragraph 2 has no topic sentence and crams ancient Mesopotamia, Morse code, and the invention of computers into one block. lexical.justification: Resource is generally adequate. You attempt some less-common items — "*timekeeping*", "*electronic signals*", "*binary digits*" — and use them correctly, which is positive. However, range is restricted: "*people*" appears 6 times in 290 words; "*develop*"/"*developed*" appears 4 times; "*things*" appears as filler. There are also collocation/word-choice slips: "*that shone*" is the wrong register, "*a full of small switches*" is malformed, and "*a single hardware*" treats *hardware* as countable. This sits in Band 6's "*restricted range or a lack of precision in word choice*" (`task2-band-descriptors.md` §Band 6).

grammar.justification: A mix of simple and complex sentences is attempted, but complex structures break repeatedly. Clear errors: "*so that no physical objects such as people or dove needed*" (fragment, missing verb, wrong article on *dove*); "*Computers consist of a full of small switches*" (broken determiner phrase); "*The reason it matters is because*" (cause-redundancy); "*not to mention the Babylonians used base-60 for timekeeping*" (subordination problem). rewrites.original: In this essay, I will discuss how mathematics influenced computer science.
rewrites.improved: While engineering breakthroughs often dominate the popular narrative, this essay will argue that the computer revolution rests fundamentally on three mathematical foundations: Boolean logic, binary representation, and the formal theory of computation.
rewrites.reason: Adds a clear, developed thesis with three categorisable strands — fixes the missing-position issue that caps TR at 5.

rewrites.original: Personally, and especially, that shone during the period of the computer revolution.
rewrites.improved: This contribution became especially visible during the mid-20th-century computer revolution, when abstract mathematical ideas were translated into physical machinery.
rewrites.reason: Removes the awkward stance marker; replaces vague "*shone*" with a precise verb-phrase; introduces a body-paragraph spine.

rewrites.original: Computers consist of a full of small switches which can turn on and off.
rewrites.improved: A modern computer is built from billions of tiny switches whose on-and-off states correspond directly to the 0s and 1s of binary arithmetic — a representation Claude Shannon formalised in 1937 by mapping Boolean algebra onto electrical circuits.
rewrites.reason: Repairs the broken determiner; adds a relative clause + dash-clause + concrete name/date. Demonstrates the Band-7 "variety of complex structures" with on-prompt content.

rewrites.original: so that no physical objects such as people or dove needed
rewrites.improved: meaning that messages no longer had to be carried physically — by couriers or homing pigeons — but could travel as patterns of electrical pulses.
rewrites.reason: Eliminates the sentence fragment; corrects collocation (*homing pigeons*); replaces vague "*needed*" with passive figure-style language.

rewrites.original: If it has billions and trillions of switches, then it can go up to almost countless numbers.
rewrites.improved: Were a processor to contain billions of such switches, it could in principle represent any number expressible in binary, however large.
rewrites.reason: Replaces childlike phrasing with an inverted second conditional + hedging — a high-leverage Band-7 grammar showcase.

rewrites.original: The reason it matters is because every letter is actually a number, and every emoji is also a number.
rewrites.improved: What makes this binary substrate so powerful is the principle of encoding: every letter, image and emoji on a screen ultimately reduces to a sequence of integers that the machine can manipulate arithmetically.
rewrites.reason: Replaces *reason…because* redundancy with a cleft sentence; introduces the technical term *encoding*.

rewrites.original: Humans have always wanted to preserve their thoughts somewhere to communicate.
rewrites.improved: Long before electronic computation, humans were already harnessing mathematics — from the Babylonian base-60 system to Egyptian geometry — to record, calculate, and communicate.
rewrites.reason: Re-anchors the paragraph on mathematics rather than communication; uses a participle-style aside; deploys topic-cluster vocabulary.

rewrites.original: This was developed even further, and people finally invented a machine which can do several things in a single hardware, a computer.
rewrites.improved: Building on this lineage, mathematicians such as Alan Turing and John von Neumann eventually formalised the idea of a programmable machine — a single device capable of executing any computable algorithm.
rewrites.reason: Replaces ambiguous *this* with a clear participle clause; supplies on-topic domain figures; corrects *a single hardware*.

convergence_note: This is iteration 1 of the chain — the prior-feedback context is empty — so per the harness rules I must mark this REFINING regardless of confidence. Future iterations should test whether TR=5 (versus a more lenient 6 on the grounds the prompt is partially answered) is the correct calibration, and should push deeper into less-obvious upgrades (inversion, cleft, passive figure verbs) if the obvious fixes converge.

# Iteration 10 — claude

_Generated 2026-05-10T10:29:34Z. Prior chain depth: up to 3. Manifest: 462 files._

---Now I have enough corpus material to produce calibrated feedback. The prior feedback chain is empty, so per the harness rules I will output REFINING regardless of confidence.

## Per-criterion scores

| Criterion | Band | Descriptor evidence (file path) |
|---|---|---|
| Task Response | 5 | `contexts/01-band-descriptors/task2-band-descriptors.md` §Band 5 — "main parts of the prompt are **incompletely addressed**"; the essay drifts to communication history, no conclusion, position underdeveloped |
| Coherence and Cohesion | 6 | `contexts/01-band-descriptors/task2-band-descriptors.md` §Band 6 — overall progression visible across three paragraphs but topic-drift, no concluding paragraph; cohesion "mechanical" and topic of paragraphs unclear |
| Lexical Resource | 6 | `contexts/01-band-descriptors/task2-band-descriptors.md` §Band 6 — "generally adequate… rather restricted range"; some specifics (*base-60, binary digits, electronic signals*) but heavy reliance on *people, things, develop* |
| Grammatical Range & Accuracy | 5 | `contexts/01-band-descriptors/task2-band-descriptors.md` §Band 5 — "complex sentences are attempted, they tend to be faulty"; fragment "*so that no physical objects such as people or dove needed*" plus several broken complex structures |
| **Overall** | **5.5** | (5+6+6+5)/4 = 5.5; per `contexts/01-band-descriptors/scoring-rules.md` §3, .50 reports as 5.5 |

## What changed from prior feedback
This is iteration 1 — there is no prior feedback to compare against. The harness instructs me to mark this an early iteration.

## Structural feedback
The single highest-impact problem is **off-topic drift**: the prompt asks about the influence of *mathematics* on computer science, but the essay is a chronological history of *communication tools* (Mesopotamia, Egypt, Morse code, then computers). Mathematics is named in the introduction and reappears only in paragraph 3 as "binary digits", but you never explain *how* mathematical ideas (Boolean logic, algorithms, numerical representation, discrete maths, Turing's formal models) drove the computer revolution. Per `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/task-response-upgrades.md` §10 ("off-topic drift"), this caps Task Response at Band 5 even before considering depth.

The essay is also **truncated** — there is no conclusion paragraph, so neither a synthesised position nor a re-statement of stance exists. Combined with the missing thesis ("*In this essay, I will discuss how mathematics influenced computer science*" announces a topic but no claim), this confirms a Band-5 TR profile (`contexts/05-band-6-to-7/task-response-upgrades.md` §4 "clear-position rule").

Paragraph shape is the second highest-impact fix: none of the body paragraphs follows PEEL (Point → Explain → Example → Link). Paragraph 2 has no topic sentence — it opens with the universal claim "*Humans have always wanted to preserve their thoughts*", drifts through Mesopotamia and Morse, and ends on the invention of the computer. Re-architecting the body around two mathematical ideas (e.g., **Boolean logic and binary representation** in body 1; **algorithms and computability** in body 2), each PEEL-shaped, will deliver more uplift than any sentence-level rewrite below.

## Focus areas

- **On-prompt content selection** — the essay's biggest leak; everything you write must answer "how did *mathematics* influence *computer science*?". Drill: `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/task-response-upgrades.md`, `contexts/10-task2-topic-banks/topic-17-science-and-research.md`.
- **PEEL paragraph structure with clear topic sentence** — none of your three body paragraphs has one. Drill: `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/paragraph-structure-band7.md`, `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/cohesion-upgrades.md` §5.
- **Article use on abstract nouns and unique entities** — "*the computer revolution*" / "*Morse code*" / "*humans*" / "*a single hardware*" all need auditing. Drill: `contexts/09-instructor-tips/10-common-korean-chinese-speaker-errors.md` §A1–A2.
- **Sentence-fragment elimination after subordinators / participles** — "*so that no physical objects such as people or dove needed*" has no main verb. Drill: `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/grammar-upgrades.md` §12, `contexts/09-instructor-tips/10-common-korean-chinese-speaker-errors.md` §I.
- **Hedging and academic register** — "*Personally, and especially, that shone*" mixes informal stance markers awkwardly. Drill: `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/vocabulary-upgrades.md` §3 (hedging toolkit).
- **Concrete domain vocabulary for the maths-CS link** — words you should be deploying but aren't: *Boolean algebra, binary representation, algorithms, computability, formal logic, abstraction, encoding, discrete mathematics, Turing's model, von Neumann architecture*. Drill: `contexts/09-instructor-tips/09-topic-vocabulary-clusters.md`.

## What's working
You attempt **specific historical illustration** ("*ancient Mesopotamia and Egypt*", "*the Babylonians used base-60 for timekeeping*", "*Samuel Morse invented Morse code in 1844*"), which is exactly the kind of concrete support `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/task-response-upgrades.md` §6 rewards — keep this habit, but anchor the examples to a mathematical claim. Your final paragraph also lands one accurate, on-topic insight: "*every letter is actually a number, and every emoji is also a number*" — that's a real Band-7-able idea about *encoding*; it just needs developing.

## Per-criterion justification

**Task Response (5).** The prompt is incompletely addressed: the essay traces communication history rather than the *mathematical* drivers of computing. You write "*Personally, and especially, that shone during the period of the computer revolution*" as a thesis, but no clear position is stated and the body never engages with mathematics until the third paragraph mentions "*binary digits*". The response is also truncated with no conclusion, leaving the reader to infer the stance — exactly the Band 5 "*position visible but underdeveloped*" pattern (`task2-band-descriptors.md` §Band 5).

**Coherence and Cohesion (6).** Information is generally arranged coherently across three paragraphs (origins → development → computers) and there is "*a clear overall progression*" (`task2-band-descriptors.md` §Band 6). However, paragraphing is faulty: paragraph 2 has no topic sentence and crams ancient Mesopotamia, Morse code, and the invention of computers into one block. Cohesion is mechanical: "*To do that*", "*to achieve that*", "*By doing that*" repeat the same referencing strategy and "*This was developed even further*" leaves "*this*" ambiguous (`contexts/05-band-6-to-7/cohesion-upgrades.md` §3 Pillar 1, §9 "demonstrative without anchor").

**Lexical Resource (6).** Resource is generally adequate. You attempt some less-common items — "*timekeeping*", "*electronic signals*", "*binary digits*" — and use them correctly, which is positive. However, range is restricted: "*people*" appears 6 times in 290 words; "*develop*"/"*developed*" appears 4 times; "*things*" appears as filler. There are also collocation/word-choice slips: "*that shone*" is the wrong register, "*a full of small switches*" is malformed, and "*a single hardware*" treats *hardware* as countable. This sits in Band 6's "*restricted range or a lack of precision in word choice*" (`task2-band-descriptors.md` §Band 6).

**Grammatical Range & Accuracy (5).** A mix of simple and complex sentences is attempted, but complex structures break repeatedly. Clear errors: "*so that no physical objects such as people or dove needed*" (fragment, missing verb, wrong article on *dove*); "*Computers consist of a full of small switches*" (broken determiner phrase); "*The reason it matters is because*" (cause-redundancy); "*not to mention the Babylonians used base-60 for timekeeping*" (subordination problem). Errors in complex sentences are systematic, matching Band 5's "*complex sentences are attempted, they tend to be faulty*" (`task2-band-descriptors.md` §Band 5). Error-free sentences are not yet *frequent*, which is the gating Band-7 feature.

## Top concrete rewrites (ranked by band uplift)

| # | Original phrase from essay | Improved (band-7 target) | Reason | Corpus citation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | "*In this essay, I will discuss how mathematics influenced computer science.*" | "*While engineering breakthroughs often dominate the popular narrative, this essay will argue that the computer revolution rests fundamentally on three mathematical foundations: Boolean logic, binary representation, and the formal theory of computation.*" | Adds a clear, developed thesis with three categorisable strands — fixes the missing-position issue that caps TR at 5. | `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/task-response-upgrades.md` §4, §7 |
| 2 | "*Personally, and especially, that shone during the period of the computer revolution.*" | "*This contribution became especially visible during the mid-20th-century computer revolution, when abstract mathematical ideas were translated into physical machinery.*" | Removes the awkward stance marker; replaces vague "*shone*" with a precise verb-phrase; introduces a body-paragraph spine. | `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/vocabulary-upgrades.md` §1 (replace vague evaluation) |
| 3 | "*Computers consist of a full of small switches which can turn on and off.*" | "*A modern computer is built from billions of tiny switches whose on-and-off states correspond directly to the 0s and 1s of binary arithmetic — a representation Claude Shannon formalised in 1937 by mapping Boolean algebra onto electrical circuits.*" | Repairs the broken determiner; adds a relative clause + dash-clause + concrete name/date. Demonstrates the Band-7 "variety of complex structures" with on-prompt content. | `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/grammar-upgrades.md` §2 (relative clauses), §5 (complex noun phrases) |
| 4 | "*so that no physical objects such as people or dove needed*" | "*meaning that messages no longer had to be carried physically — by couriers or homing pigeons — but could travel as patterns of electrical pulses.*" | Eliminates the sentence fragment; corrects collocation (*homing pigeons*); replaces vague "*needed*" with passive figure-style language. | `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/grammar-upgrades.md` §12 (punctuation, fragments); `contexts/09-instructor-tips/04-passive-voice-patterns.md` |
| 5 | "*If it has billions and trillions of switches, then it can go up to almost countless numbers.*" | "*Were a processor to contain billions of such switches, it could in principle represent any number expressible in binary, however large.*" | Replaces childlike phrasing with an inverted second conditional + hedging — a high-leverage Band-7 grammar showcase. | `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/grammar-upgrades.md` §3.1 (inverted conditionals); `contexts/09-instructor-tips/07-band7-grammar-showcases.md` |
| 6 | "*The reason it matters is because every letter is actually a number, and every emoji is also a number.*" | "*What makes this binary substrate so powerful is the principle of encoding: every letter, image and emoji on a screen ultimately reduces to a sequence of integers that the machine can manipulate arithmetically.*" | Replaces *reason…because* redundancy with a cleft sentence; introduces the technical term *encoding*. | `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/grammar-upgrades.md` §8 (cleft sentences); `contexts/09-instructor-tips/09-topic-vocabulary-clusters.md` |
| 7 | "*Humans have always wanted to preserve their thoughts somewhere to communicate.*" | "*Long before electronic computation, humans were already harnessing mathematics — from the Babylonian base-60 system to Egyptian geometry — to record, calculate, and communicate.*" | Re-anchors the paragraph on mathematics rather than communication; uses a participle-style aside; deploys topic-cluster vocabulary. | `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/task-response-upgrades.md` §10 (off-topic drift); `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/vocabulary-upgrades.md` §4.0 |
| 8 | "*This was developed even further, and people finally invented a machine which can do several things in a single hardware, a computer.*" | "*Building on this lineage, mathematicians such as Alan Turing and John von Neumann eventually formalised the idea of a programmable machine — a single device capable of executing any computable algorithm.*" | Replaces ambiguous *this* with a clear participle clause; supplies on-topic domain figures; corrects *a single hardware*. | `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/grammar-upgrades.md` §4 (participle clauses); `contexts/05-band-6-to-7/cohesion-upgrades.md` §9 |

## Overall band rationale
Per `contexts/01-band-descriptors/scoring-rules.md` §3, the criterion average (5+6+6+5)/4 = 5.50 reports as Band 5.5. The off-topic drift and missing conclusion pull TR down to 5; complex-sentence breakage pulls GRA to 5; CC and LR remain at Band 6.

## CONVERGENCE: REFINING
This is iteration 1 of the chain — the prior-feedback context is empty — so per the harness rules I must mark this REFINING regardless of confidence. Future iterations should test whether TR=5 (versus a more lenient 6 on the grounds the prompt is partially answered) is the correct calibration, and should push deeper into less-obvious upgrades (inversion, cleft, passive figure verbs) if the obvious fixes converge.
